tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
A YOUNG ASTRONOMER

My name is Priya, and I am thirteen years old. Most of my friends like football or video games, but my favourite thing in the world is staring at the night sky. People sometimes call me a dreamer, yet I prefer the word astronomer, because I really do study the stars.

It all began three years ago, on the night of my tenth birthday. My grandmother gave me an old pair of binoculars that had once belonged to her father. That evening, we walked out into the garden and she pointed up at a faint, milky band of light stretching across the sky. "That," she whispered, "is our home, the Milky Way." I felt a shiver run down my spine. From that moment, I was hooked.

At first, finding anything was almost impossible. The sky looked like a chaotic mess of tiny dots. Slowly, though, I learned to recognise patterns called constellations, and the dots began to make sense. My favourite is Orion, the hunter, with his bright belt of three stars in a neat row. Last winter I finally spotted Jupiter, and I could even see four of its moons through my binoculars.

The biggest problem where I live is light pollution. The glow from streetlamps and houses hides all but the brightest stars. To escape it, my parents sometimes drive me out to the countryside, far from any town. There, on a clear, moonless night, the sky is absolutely breathtaking, and thousands of stars suddenly appear.

I keep a notebook of everything I observe, with the date, the time, and a small sketch of what I saw. One day I hope to study astrophysics at university and perhaps work for a space agency. For now, though, I am happy simply to lie on the grass, look up, and feel tiny beneath the enormous, glittering dome above me.

items.statement: Priya's interest in the stars started on her tenth birthday.
items.explanation: TRUE — the text says it all began "three years ago, on the night of my tenth birthday", when her grandmother gave her the binoculars.

items.statement: Priya bought her binoculars in a shop.
items.explanation: FALSE — the binoculars were a gift from her grandmother: they "had once belonged to her father", not something Priya bought.

items.statement: Priya saw some of Jupiter's moons through her binoculars.
items.explanation: TRUE — "Last winter I finally spotted Jupiter, and I could even see four of its moons through my binoculars."

items.statement: Priya's grandmother is also a keen astronomer who observes the sky every night.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — the grandmother gave Priya the binoculars and pointed out the Milky Way once, but the text never says whether she is herself an astronomer or observes the sky regularly.

items.statement: Light pollution makes it hard to see most stars from where Priya lives.
items.explanation: TRUE — "The biggest problem where I live is light pollution. The glow from streetlamps and houses hides all but the brightest stars."

items.statement: Priya travels to the countryside by train to get away from the city lights.
items.explanation: FALSE — her parents "drive me out to the countryside", so they go by car, not by train.

vocabItems.definition: a pattern of stars in the sky that has been given a name
not bright; difficult to see clearly
a small, rough drawing done quickly
very strongly attracted to something and unable to stop doing or wanting it
